title: Male Hypogonadism: 30 Literature-Based Case Summaries
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: primary vs secondary vs mixed classification, congenital and acquired causes (Kallmann, empty sella, opioids, hemochromatosis, 46,XX DSD, PAIS, CAH, mixed KS + pituitary adenoma), fertility pathways (hCG/FSH/pulsatile GnRH), and 18 board-style MCQs — educational revision from published case reports (R1–R24)
authors: Resident training module — 30 published case summaries (see sourceFile references R1–R24)
sourceFile: Literature cases R1–R24 (PMC/PubMed links in module); StatPearls NBK532933; Endotext NBK279078, NBK279076, NBK279160

patient.chiefComplaint: Delayed puberty, infertility, low testosterone, or hypogonadal symptoms — 30 published cases
pmh: Cases span congenital HH, Kallmann, empty sella, opioids, hemochromatosis, prolactinoma, 46,XX DSD, PAIS, CAH/TART, and mixed KS + pituitary macroadenoma

caseDescription: A resident revision module synthesising 30 published male hypogonadism cases (references R1–R24). Section A: congenital/syndromic/idiopathic HH — normosmic IHH, Kallmann (KAL1/ANOS1), fertile eunuch variant, normosmic-asymmetric anosmia, RNF216, 22q11.2 mixed hypogonadism, and Klinefelter in series. Section B: acquired secondary — empty sella, opioids, soy/isoflavones, hemochromatosis (reversible with phlebotomy), prolactinoma with hCG fertility rescue. Section C: primary/DSD/mixed — 46,XX SRY-positive male, PAIS, classical CAH (reversible vs TART irreversible), Klinefelter plus pituitary macroadenoma. Ultra-quick approach: confirm low morning testosterone ×2, classify by LH/FSH, work up central causes, remember testosterone improves virilization but not fertility (use hCG ± FSH or pulsatile GnRH).
keyFindings: Primary = low T + high LH/FSH; secondary = low T + low/inappropriately normal LH/FSH
Anosmia + delayed puberty → Kallmann until proven otherwise
Fertility in HH → hCG ± FSH or pulsatile GnRH, not testosterone alone
Iron studies, prolactin, drug/exposure history essential in central hypogonadism
Discordant gonadotropins (e.g. KS + normal LH) → look for second pituitary lesion
finalDiagnosis: Male hypogonadism — a syndromic classification exercise across 30 literature cases spanning primary testicular failure, secondary hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, and mixed patterns, with emphasis on reversible causes (opioids, iron overload, soy, optimised CAH) versus irreversible (TART destruction, long-standing KS) and fertility-preserving treatment pathways.
learningObjectives: Apply the two-sample morning testosterone rule and LH/FSH-based classification
Recognise Kallmann, IHH, empty sella, opioid, and hemochromatosis patterns from published vignettes
Choose fertility-preserving therapy (hCG/FSH/pulsatile GnRH) versus testosterone replacement
Identify PAIS, 46,XX male DSD, and mixed hypogonadism from discordant clinical and lab clues
Execute practical secondary hypogonadism workup including prolactin, iron studies, and pituitary MRI

items.title: How to approach a man with suspected hypogonadism
items.content: Step 1: Confirm low early-morning total testosterone on two separate occasions. Step 2: Classify — primary (testicular) = low T + high LH/FSH; secondary/central = low T + low or inappropriately normal LH/FSH; mixed = evidence of both testicular and hypothalamic-pituitary disease. Step 3: In secondary hypogonadism consider Kallmann/congenital HH, pituitary tumours/prolactinoma, empty sella/hypophysitis, hemochromatosis, opioids, exogenous androgens, severe illness, obesity, sleep disorders. Step 4: If fertility desired — testosterone improves virilization but does NOT restore spermatogenesis; use pulsatile GnRH or hCG ± FSH. Step 5: Workup often includes prolactin, iron studies (ferritin/transferrin saturation), thyroid and adrenal axes, pituitary MRI when indicated.

items.title: Why testosterone replacement does not restore fertility in hypogonadotropic hypogonadism
items.content: Exogenous testosterone suppresses pituitary LH and FSH through negative feedback, further inhibiting spermatogenesis. Men with congenital or acquired HH who desire fertility need direct gonadal stimulation with hCG (LH receptor mimic on Leydig cells) and often FSH or hMG for Sertoli/germ-cell support, or pulsatile GnRH when the pituitary-gonadal axis is intact (Cases 4, 6, 17, 23–24). Prior testosterone therapy can cause azoospermia — gonadotropin rescue may still succeed (Case 4). Virilization and fertility are separate treatment goals.
keyPoints: hCG ± FSH or pulsatile GnRH for fertility.

items.title: Case 1: Normosmic idiopathic HH (IHH)
items.content: 20-year-old man: absent secondary sexual characteristics, feminine voice. Profoundly low testosterone, suppressed LH/FSH, 46,XY, no pituitary cause on imaging. Pattern: secondary hypogonadism. Pearl: Very low T with very low gonadotropins and normal imaging strongly suggests congenital/idiopathic HH. Ref: R1 (Lodh & Mukhopadhyay, PMC4731360).

items.title: Case 2: IHH with short Y chromosome
items.content: 18-year-old: obesity, gynecomastia, absent hair, delayed bone age, small testes. Very low T, LH, FSH; normal MRI; cytogenetics showed small Y chromosome. Pattern: secondary. Pearl: Chromosomal variant present but hormonal pattern still guides classification. Ref: R1.

items.title: Why low testosterone with high LH/FSH indicates primary testicular failure (Case 3 — Klinefelter)
items.content: 21-year-old: sparse body hair, gynecomastia, small testes, low T, elevated LH/FSH. Karyotype 47,XXY. Pattern: primary hypogonadism. Pearl: Low T + high LH/FSH + small firm testes = classic Klinefelter. Ref: R1.

items.title: Why congenital HH fertility requires gonadotropins, not testosterone alone (Case 4)
items.content: 21-year-old congenital HH: absent puberty until 16, very low gonadotropins/T, normal smell, unilateral cryptorchidism, normal pituitary/olfactory MRI. Prior testosterone caused azoospermia; combined gonadotropin treatment produced sperm. Pattern: secondary. Pearl: Fertility needs hCG and often FSH, not testosterone alone. Ref: R2 (Salvio et al., PMC12112936).

items.title: Case 5: Acquired HH from probable hypophysitis
items.content: 33-year-old previously fertile man: secondary infertility, low libido, ED, azoospermia. Low LH/FSH/T; MRI pituitary stalk enlargement suggestive of hypophysitis. hCG then FSH produced sperm for ART. Pattern: secondary. Pearl: Adult-onset low T with low gonadotropins in a previously fertile man → pituitary imaging. Ref: R2.

items.title: Why pulsatile GnRH is physiologic therapy in KAL1 Kallmann (Case 6)
items.content: 26-year-old delayed puberty. Dynamic testing: delayed LH/FSH secretion. KAL1 mutation. Pulsatile subcutaneous GnRH improved gonadotropins and testosterone. Pattern: congenital secondary. Pearl: Pulsatile GnRH is physiologic when pituitary-gonadal unit is intact. Ref: R3 (Zhang & Fu, PMC9477064).

items.title: Why Kallmann syndrome may present first as adult primary infertility (Case 7)
items.content: 34-year-old primary infertility: gynecomastia, Tanner G2, anosmia, small testes, small prostate. MRI: olfactory bulb agenesis/hypoplastic sulci. Pattern: congenital secondary. Pearl: Kallmann can be missed until adulthood when infertility is the presenting complaint. Ref: R4 (PMC9733469).

items.title: Case 8: Kallmann with incidental pituitary microadenoma
items.content: 17-year-old: eunuchoid proportions, micropenis, corrected cryptorchidism, absent secondary sexual characteristics, anosmia. MRI: bilateral olfactory bulb agenesis + nonfunctioning 3.5 mm microadenoma. Pattern: congenital secondary. Pearl: Small pituitary lesion does not exclude Kallmann if phenotypic and olfactory features are typical. Ref: R5.

items.title: Case 9: Kallmann later complicated by prolactinomas
items.content: Kallmann diagnosed at 15 (absent puberty, very small testes, micropenis, osteopenia, anosmia). At 26: headache; MRI two separate pituitary adenomas; hyperprolactinemia responded to cabergoline but hypogonadism persisted. Pattern: congenital HH + acquired prolactinoma. Pearl: One patient can have congenital HH and later acquire additional pituitary hypogonadism. Ref: R6 (PMC10642931).

items.title: Case 10: ANOS1 Kallmann — affected brother 1
items.content: Hispanic brother: anosmia, delayed puberty, azoospermia, undetectable LH/FSH. WES: pathogenic stop mutation in ANOS1. Pattern: X-linked congenital secondary. Pearl: Family history converts 'idiopathic' HH into defined genetic diagnosis. Ref: R7.

items.title: Case 11: ANOS1 Kallmann — affected brother 2
items.content: Second brother: same anosmia, delayed puberty, azoospermia, suppressed gonadotropins — confirms familial X-linked disease. Pattern: X-linked congenital secondary. Pearl: Test relatives — inheritance and fertility counselling change once mutation is known. Ref: R7.

items.title: Case 12: Fertile eunuch variant of Kallmann
items.content: Seen at 16 for delayed puberty; as adult low T and low gonadotropins but normal sperm count and complete anosmia. Pattern: partial/incomplete HH. Pearl: Complete anosmia does not necessarily predict complete gonadotropin failure. Ref: R8.

items.title: Case 13: Kallmann with apparently preserved smell
items.content: 39-year-old HH features but overall smell seemed preserved. Detailed testing: unilateral anosmia, contralateral normosmia; MRI unilateral olfactory aplasia. Pattern: congenital secondary. Pearl: 'Normosmic Kallmann' can be asymmetric olfactory pathway defect. Ref: R9.

items.title: Case 14: RNF216 Gordon Holmes spectrum
items.content: 17-year-old: absent secondary sexual characteristics, high-pitched voice, Tanner G1/P1, gynecomastia. Low gonadotropins and low T; biallelic RNF216 variants (disorder with later neurodegeneration). Pattern: syndromic congenital secondary. Pearl: HH can be first sign of progressive neuroendocrine syndrome in adolescents. Ref: R10.

items.title: Case 15: 22q11.2 deletion — mixed hypogonadism
items.content: 35-year-old: recurrent infections, learning difficulty, obesity, cataract, hypocalcemia, renal/cardiac disease, then hypogonadism. Rare combination of primary testicular failure + decreased pituitary gonadotropin reserve. Pattern: mixed. Pearl: Hypogonadism + hypocalcemia → reconsider congenital syndromic disease even in adulthood. Ref: R11.

items.title: Case 16: Partial empty sella — prepubertal presentation
items.content: 20-year-old: micropenis, absent secondary sexual characteristics. MRI partial empty sella; low T, LH, FSH, estradiol, β-hCG. Pattern: secondary. Pearl: Empty sella can present through gonadal axis in younger males. Ref: R12 (PMC7784113).

items.title: How pulsatile GnRH restored fertility in empty sella (Case 17)
items.content: 45-year-old: fatigue, decreased libido. LH 0.02, FSH 0.03 mIU/mL, T 0.72 nmol/L, MRI empty sella. Pulsatile GnRH improved symptoms, normalised hormones, improved sperm motility, natural conception. Pattern: secondary. Pearl: Selected responsive axes can achieve fertility with physiologic GnRH. Ref: R13.

items.title: Case 18: Low T revealing partial empty sella and panhypopituitarism
items.content: 56-year-old: low libido, chronic hyponatremia, low T, low-normal LH/FSH, low free T4, low morning cortisol, low IGF-1 → partial empty sella. Pattern: secondary as part of broader hypopituitarism. Pearl: Low T + unexplained hyponatremia → evaluate cortisol and thyroid axes, not androgen replacement alone. Ref: R14 (PMC10823205).

items.title: Why chronic opioids cause reversible hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (Case 19)
items.content: 42-year-old on transdermal fentanyl for chronic pain: fatigue, ED. Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism; normal brain MRI — opioid effect likely. Pattern: secondary. Pearl: Chronic opioids are a reversible cause of low testosterone. Ref: R15.

items.title: How excess soy/isoflavone intake caused secondary hypogonadism (Case 20)
items.content: 54-year-old drank ~1.2 L soy milk daily for 3 years: ED, gynecomastia. Profoundly suppressed LH/FSH, T 12.6 ng/dL; improved after stopping soy. Pattern: secondary. Pearl: Drug/diet history — unusual exposures can mimic pituitary disease. Ref: R16 (PMC9593161).

items.title: How phlebotomy restored fertility in hemochromatosis-related HH (Case 21)
items.content: 37-year-old idiopathic hemochromatosis: impotence, low libido, azoospermia, HH. After aggressive iron depletion, LH/FSH/T normalised; later fathered a child. Pattern: secondary. Pearl: Iron overload is classic reversible HH if treated early. Ref: R17.

items.title: Case 22: Hemochromatosis — combined hypothalamic and pituitary injury
items.content: Adult hemochromatosis: low T, inappropriately normal LH, normal T rise after hCG, normal pituitary LH response to GnRH, abnormal clomiphene response — hypothalamic dysfunction + pituitary involvement. Pattern: secondary. Pearl: Iron can injure more than one level of reproductive axis. Ref: R18.

items.title: Case 23: Post-operative prolactinoma — hCG fertility rescue
items.content: 28-year-old prolactinoma: persistent hyperprolactinemia and hypogonadism after surgery. Testosterone for virilization; later hCG/hMG → sperm production; partner delivered healthy child. Pattern: secondary. Pearl: Downstream gonadal stimulation can achieve fertility when central output remains poor. Ref: R19.

items.title: Case 24: Second prolactinoma patient — hCG response
items.content: Young man with prolactinoma and HH: hCG achieved rapid genital maturation and normal semen parameters. Pattern: secondary. Pearl: hCG restores virilization and sometimes spermatogenesis despite abnormal central gonadotropin secretion. Ref: R19.

items.title: Why karyotype is mandatory in hypergonadotropic azoospermia (Case 25 — 46,XX male)
items.content: 24-year-old: ED, low libido, gynecomastia, testes 6 mL, azoospermia, osteopenia, hypergonadotropic hypogonadism. Karyotype 46,XX SRY-positive male DSD. Pattern: primary. Pearl: Phenotypic male with small testes, azoospermia, hypergonadotropic pattern → karyotype. Ref: R20 (PMC11455242).

items.title: Case 26: 46,XX male DSD — adolescent gynecomastia
items.content: 13-year-old: progressive bilateral gynecomastia, small testes, elevated LH/FSH, low T. FISH: SRY translocation to X chromosome. Pattern: primary. Pearl: 46,XX male DSD may present first as pubertal gynecomastia. Ref: R21 (PMC6958519).

items.title: Why high testosterone with high LH indicates androgen resistance, not hypogonadism (Case 27 — PAIS)
items.content: 16-year-old: prior hypospadias repair, gynecomastia, micropenis. High testosterone, elevated LH, elevated estradiol, 46,XY, AR mutation. Pattern: androgen resistance — not classic T deficiency. Pearl: High T + high LH in undervirilized male → androgen resistance. Ref: R22 (PMC4508269).

items.title: How optimising glucocorticoids restored spermatogenesis in classical CAH (Case 28)
items.content: Male classical CAH, 18 months infertility: high ACTH. Intensified glucocorticoid treatment normalised semen analysis; three spontaneous partner pregnancies followed. Pattern: functional gonadal suppression. Pearl: Poor CAH control may impair spermatogenesis — sometimes reverses with better therapy. Ref: R23 (PMC6532123).

items.title: Why large TART lesions cause irreversible azoospermia (Case 29)
items.content: Male classical CAH: 2 years infertility, extensive testicular adrenal rest tissue occupying most of testis, hypergonadotropic hypogonadism, persistent azoospermia despite treatment intensification. Pattern: primary testicular damage/mixed. Pearl: Large TART lesions can destroy parenchyma irreversibly. Ref: R23.

items.title: Why discordant LH in Klinefelter syndrome demands pituitary MRI (Case 30)
items.content: 37-year-old tall man: obesity, low libido, incomplete puberty, testes <4 mL, T 43 ng/dL, elevated FSH, but only inappropriately normal LH. Karyotype 47,XXY + pituitary macroadenoma 8×7×14 mm. Pattern: mixed primary + secondary. Pearl: Gonadotropins not fitting pure primary failure → look for additional pituitary lesion. Ref: R24 (PMC11453621).

items.title: Why two early-morning testosterone samples are mandatory before diagnosis
items.content: Diurnal variation, acute illness, obesity, and laboratory variability can produce a single spuriously low testosterone. Guidelines require confirmation on two separate early-morning (7–10 AM) fasting samples before diagnosing hypogonadism. Only then does LH/FSH classification (primary vs secondary vs mixed) direct the workup meaningfully. Starting testosterone or fertility therapy without this confirmation risks treating transient suppression (illness, opioids, obesity) as permanent disease.

items.title: High-yield revision points from all 30 cases
items.content: Anosmia + delayed puberty = Kallmann until proven otherwise. Low T + low/normal LH/FSH = central cause (unless acute illness/drug). Low T + high LH/FSH = primary failure (Klinefelter, 46,XX male, TART). Fertility in HH = hCG ± FSH or pulsatile GnRH, not testosterone alone. Ask about opioids, exogenous androgens, boosters, extreme diets. Iron studies in unexplained central hypogonadism. Prolactin in low libido/ED with low T. Pituitary disease may be broader — check cortisol, free T4. Karyotype in primary hypogonadism with small testes. Discordant labs (KS + normal LH) → second pituitary lesion.

items.title: Why iron studies and prolactin are mandatory in unexplained central hypogonadism
items.content: Cases 21–22 show hemochromatosis as reversible HH with phlebotomy — missed if ferritin/transferrin saturation are not checked. Cases 9, 23–24 show prolactinoma coexisting with or causing hypogonadism — prolactin is essential in any man with low libido, ED, and low testosterone. Case 18 shows 'low T' as the entry point to panhypopituitarism with hyponatremia. A testosterone-centric workup without iron, prolactin, and other pituitary axes is incomplete.

items.title: Why discordant gonadotropins demand pituitary imaging even when karyotype explains partial picture
items.content: Case 30: known 47,XXY with high FSH (expected in primary failure) but LH only 'normal' — not the expected high LH pattern. Pituitary macroadenoma 8×7×14 mm found. Case 9: Kallmann plus later prolactinomas. Lesson: when gonadotropins do not fit the textbook pattern for a known diagnosis, image the pituitary — dual pathology is documented in literature and changes management.

items.question: Why must hypogonadism be classified as primary versus secondary before choosing therapy?
items.answer: Because the diagnostic pathway, imaging, genetic testing, and fertility options differ completely. Primary (high LH/FSH) directs to testicular causes — karyotype, testicular ultrasound, TART in CAH. Secondary (low/inappropriately normal LH/FSH) directs to pituitary/hypothalamic causes — MRI, prolactin, iron studies, drug history, pulsatile GnRH or hCG for fertility. Mixed patterns (Cases 15, 30) require both pathways. Misclassification leads to wrong imaging, missed reversible causes, and fertility plans that fail.

items.question: How should fertility be induced in a man with congenital hypogonadotropic hypogonadism who previously received testosterone?
items.answer: Stop or avoid further exogenous testosterone. Use hCG to stimulate Leydig cells (testosterone production and intratesticular androgen environment), add FSH or hMG if spermatogenesis does not resume (Sertoli/germ-cell support). If pituitary-gonadal axis is intact (e.g. KAL1 Kallmann, empty sella Case 17), pulsatile GnRH is physiologic alternative. Case 4 achieved sperm after prior testosterone-induced azoospermia with combined gonadotropins. Monitor semen analysis; ART if needed. Genetic counselling for congenital causes.

items.stem: A 17-year-old boy has delayed puberty, micropenis, absent facial hair, and anosmia. The most likely diagnosis is:
items.explanation: Delayed puberty plus anosmia is classic Kallmann syndrome (Cases 6–13). Klinefelter has high LH/FSH without anosmia.

items.stem: A man has low morning testosterone on two occasions with low LH and low FSH. This pattern best fits:
items.explanation: Low T with low/inappropriately normal gonadotropins = secondary (central) hypogonadism — Cases 1, 4–6, 16–20.

items.stem: A tall man with infertility, small firm testes, gynecomastia, low testosterone, and elevated LH/FSH most likely has:
items.explanation: Classic primary pattern — Case 3 and Case 30 (KS with additional pituitary lesion).

items.stem: In a man with congenital hypogonadotropic hypogonadism who desires fertility, the most appropriate treatment is usually:
options: hCG with or without FSH, or pulsatile GnRH
items.explanation: Testosterone suppresses spermatogenesis (Cases 4, 23). hCG ± FSH or pulsatile GnRH (Cases 6, 17) restore fertility.

items.stem: Which exposure is a well-described reversible cause of central hypogonadism in men?
items.explanation: Case 19: transdermal fentanyl caused reversible hypogonadotropic hypogonadism.

items.stem: A man with low testosterone and unexplained chronic hyponatremia should be evaluated for:
items.explanation: Case 18: partial empty sella with low cortisol, low free T4, low IGF-1 — hyponatremia signals hypopituitarism.

items.stem: Which pair most strongly suggests primary hypogonadism?
items.explanation: Textbook primary testicular failure — Cases 3, 25, 26, 29.

items.stem: The most appropriate screening test for iron-overload-related hypogonadism is:
items.explanation: Cases 21–22: hemochromatosis causes HH; Case 21 reversed with phlebotomy.

items.stem: A phenotypic male with azoospermia, small testes, hypergonadotropic hypogonadism, and 46,XX SRY-positive karyotype has:
items.explanation: Cases 25–26: 46,XX male with SRY translocation — primary hypergonadotropic pattern.

items.stem: A teenage boy has gynecomastia, micropenis, prior hypospadias repair, high testosterone, high LH, and an androgen receptor mutation. The best diagnosis is:
items.explanation: Case 27 and cr-044 Case 1: high T + high LH + undervirilization = PAIS.

items.stem: What is the major fertility-related drawback of giving exogenous testosterone to a man with secondary hypogonadism?
options: It raises prolactin in all men
It always causes polycythemia within 1 week
items.explanation: Exogenous testosterone suppresses pituitary LH/FSH — Case 4 azoospermia after prior TRT.

items.stem: Which symptom cluster should strongly suggest prolactinoma-related hypogonadism in a male?
items.explanation: Cases 9, 23–24: prolactinoma with hypogonadism — check prolactin in low libido/ED with low T.

items.stem: In Kallmann syndrome, MRI may show:
items.explanation: Cases 7, 8, 13: olfactory bulb agenesis/hypoplasia or unilateral aplasia on MRI.

items.stem: A man with known Klinefelter syndrome has very low testosterone and high FSH, but LH is only normal. The next important step is:
options: Ignore the result because Klinefelter explains everything
Pituitary imaging for a second central process
items.explanation: Case 30: discordant LH led to discovery of pituitary macroadenoma superimposed on 47,XXY.

items.stem: Excess soy/isoflavone intake in the reported case produced:
items.explanation: Case 20: 1.2 L soy milk daily — suppressed LH/FSH, improved after cessation (R16).

items.stem: Which condition in the case series was shown to be potentially reversible with phlebotomy alone?
items.explanation: Case 21: hormones and fertility recovered after aggressive iron depletion.

items.stem: A man with CAH and large testicular adrenal rest tumors is at risk for:
items.explanation: Case 29: extensive TART — persistent azoospermia despite treatment; contrast Case 28 reversible with glucocorticoids.

items.stem: The best single phrase to describe the lab pattern of most opioid-induced androgen deficiency is:
items.explanation: Case 19: hypogonadotropic hypogonadism — central suppression pattern.

items.statement: Anosmia with delayed puberty should prompt evaluation for Kallmann syndrome.
items.explanation: True. Central revision point and Cases 6–13 across the module.

items.statement: Testosterone replacement alone is sufficient to restore fertility in hypogonadotropic hypogonadism.
items.explanation: False. TRT suppresses spermatogenesis — Cases 4, 23; use hCG ± FSH or pulsatile GnRH.

items.statement: Hemochromatosis-related hypogonadotropic hypogonadism may reverse with iron depletion.
items.explanation: True. Case 21 recovered hormones and fathered a child after phlebotomy.

items.statement: Partial androgen insensitivity typically presents with low testosterone and low LH.
items.explanation: False. Case 27: high testosterone, high LH — androgen resistance with feedback escape.

items.statement: A pituitary microadenoma on MRI excludes Kallmann syndrome.
items.explanation: False. Case 8: Kallmann with incidental nonfunctioning microadenoma — olfactory features define Kallmann.

items.statement: Optimising glucocorticoid therapy in classical CAH can sometimes restore spermatogenesis.
items.explanation: True. Case 28: intensified glucocorticoids normalised semen and led to pregnancies.

items.assertion: A man with congenital HH who desires fertility should be offered hCG-based therapy rather than testosterone monotherapy.
items.reason: Exogenous testosterone suppresses pituitary gonadotropins and inhibits spermatogenesis, whereas hCG stimulates Leydig cells directly.
items.explanation: Both true — reason explains assertion. Cases 4, 17, 23–24 demonstrate successful gonadotropin pathways.

items.assertion: Pituitary MRI is indicated when a patient with known primary hypogonadism has a discordant gonadotropin pattern.
items.reason: A superimposed pituitary lesion can alter LH secretion independently of primary testicular failure, as in Klinefelter syndrome with macroadenoma.
items.explanation: Both true — Case 30: 47,XXY plus macroadenoma discovered because LH was only normal despite high FSH.
